summary: Gruber uses Spotify's decision to brick all Car Thing devices — its proprietary hardware that only supported Spotify — as a lens to examine the hypocrisy in Spotify's anti-App Store crusade. He argues that Spotify built a closed hardware platform with zero third-party support while simultaneously lobbying the EU to force Apple and Google to give Spotify commission-free access to their platforms. He traces the history of the App Store's commission structure back to 2008, when iPhone had just 1.1% market share, demonstrating that Apple's fees predated its dominance rather than being imposed after achieving it. He concludes that Spotify's position is internally contradictory: it's fine for Spotify to create a completely closed platform, but not fine for Apple to charge commissions on an open one.
key_points: Spotify is bricking all Car Thing devices with no refund, no open-sourcing, and no replacement planned — telling customers to just recycle the hardware
Car Thing never supported any music service other than Spotify's own, despite Spotify's decade-long campaign for commission-free access to Apple and Google's platforms
Apple's App Store commission structure was established in 2008 when iPhone had only 1.1% market share — the fees preceded dominance, not the other way around
Spotify built its mobile success through the App Store at a time when iPhone had single-digit market share, and Apple approved Spotify despite it being called an 'iTunes killer'
Spotify's position amounts to: closed platforms are fine when Spotify builds them, but open platforms that charge commissions become unacceptable once they're popular
money_quotes: No word in Spotify's Car Thing bricking FAQ about when they're dropping support for Apple Music, Amazon Music, and YouTube Music. Oh, that's right, they never supported any music services other than their own.
What do you think Spotify planned to do if Car Thing became a hit product? Do you think they planned to open it up to competing streaming services after it became popular? I doubt it.
And if you think they not only would have opened Car Thing up to competing services, but would have done so without charging significant commissions or fees, I have a bridge to sell you.
Spotify's anti-App-Store rhetoric would lead you to believe that Apple only began extracting 30/15 percent commissions from in-app subscriptions after the iPhone became a dominant platform.
All of the iPhone's subsequent success happened with that App Store commission in place, and that commission has only gone down over time.

It's OK — for them at least — to create a new hardware platform with no support at all for third-party software, but not OK for another company that owns its own music service to create a hardware platform that offers access to any and all competing services, but charges a commission for access, if that platform becomes popular.
I was wrong only in thinking that allowing Spotify into the App Store could avoid antitrust scrutiny from the EU.
key_insight: Spotify's willingness to build and then brick a completely closed hardware platform while demanding commission-free access to Apple's open one reveals the self-serving inconsistency at the heart of its antitrust arguments.
